skills.name: Game Physics
skills.description: Simulation of real-world physical systems including motion, forces, and collisions to create believable interactions between objects in a game environment.
skills.notes: Game physics covers rigid body dynamics, collision detection (broad and narrow phase), forces like gravity and friction, constraints, and sometimes soft body or fluid simulation. It ensures objects behave naturally and respond correctly to player actions.

skills.name: Linear Algebra
skills.description: Mathematical foundation for handling vectors, matrices, and transformations used to represent and manipulate objects in 2D and 3D space.
skills.notes: Essential for graphics and physics, linear algebra enables operations like translation, rotation, scaling, and projection. Concepts such as dot product, cross product, coordinate systems, and matrix multiplication are heavily used in game engines.

skills.name: Computer Graphics
skills.description: Techniques and systems used to generate, render, and display visual content in games, including real-time rendering pipelines and shading models.
skills.notes: Includes understanding the graphics pipeline, shaders (vertex/fragment), lighting models (Phong, PBR), textures, reflections, shadows, and post-processing effects. It is key to making games visually appealing and performant.

skills.name: Game Engine
skills.description: Software framework that provides core systems and tools for building, managing, and running games efficiently.
skills.notes: Game engines like Unity, Unreal Engine, and Godot offer built-in systems for rendering, physics, input handling, scripting, and asset pipelines. Understanding how to use and extend an engine is crucial for rapid development.

skills.name: Programming Languages
skills.description: Languages used to implement gameplay mechanics, engine systems, and performance-critical features in game development.
skills.notes: Common languages include C++ (performance-critical systems), C# (Unity scripting), Python (tools and prototyping), and Rust (safe systems programming). Mastery of programming concepts like OOP, memory management, and data structures is essential.

skills.name: Game AI
skills.description: Systems that control non-player characters (NPCs) and enable intelligent, adaptive behavior in games.
skills.notes: Includes techniques like finite state machines, behavior trees, pathfinding (A*), decision-making systems, and machine learning approaches. Game AI focuses on creating believable and fun behaviors rather than perfect intelligence.

skills.name: Advanced Rendering
skills.description: High-end rendering techniques used to achieve realistic lighting, materials, and visual effects in modern games.
skills.notes: Covers physically-based rendering (PBR), global illumination, ray tracing, reflections, volumetric effects, and advanced shadowing techniques. These methods push realism but require optimization for real-time performance.
